title: Quiz: Decoherence
description: Test your understanding of decoherence in quantum systems

questions.question: What is decoherence?

options.text: The collapse of quantum state upon measurement
The loss of quantum coherence due to environmental interaction
The creation of entanglement between qubits
The process of quantum error correction

questions.explanation: Decoherence is the process by which a quantum system loses its coherence (the phase relationships in superposition) through interaction with its environment. This is distinct from measurement collapse.

questions.question: In a density matrix, which elements represent quantum coherence?
questions.explanation: The off-diagonal elements of a density matrix (ρ₁₂ and ρ₂₁) represent quantum coherence. When these go to zero, the state becomes a classical mixture. The diagonal elements represent populations (probabilities).

questions.question: What is T2 in quantum computing?

options.text: The time for a qubit to relax from |1⟩ to |0⟩
The time for a quantum gate operation
The time for phase coherence to decay
The time between error correction cycles

questions.explanation: T2 is the dephasing time - it measures how long phase coherence in a superposition is maintained. T1 is the relaxation time for energy decay from |1⟩ to |0⟩.

questions.question: T2 can be larger than 2T1.
questions.explanation: False. T2 ≤ 2T1 always holds. This is because dephasing includes contributions from relaxation (T1 processes) plus pure dephasing. The inequality T2 ≤ 2T1 is a fundamental constraint.

questions.question: Why don't we observe quantum superpositions in everyday macroscopic objects?

options.text: Quantum mechanics doesn't apply to large objects
Decoherence times are extremely short for macroscopic objects
Large objects cannot be put in superposition

questions.explanation: Macroscopic objects have extremely short decoherence times because they interact with countless environmental particles. The decoherence time for a dust grain at room temperature is effectively instantaneous (~10⁻³⁰ seconds or less).

questions.question: Which technique can extend effective coherence time?
questions.explanation: Dynamical decoupling techniques like spin echo apply carefully timed pulses to refocus dephasing and extend effective coherence time. This is borrowed from NMR techniques.

questions.question: Which are valid strategies for fighting decoherence? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Cryogenic cooling reduces thermal noise, quantum error correction actively fixes errors, and running algorithms faster completes computation before decoherence sets in. Simply increasing qubit count doesn't help unless coherence is also improved.

questions.question: How does decoherence fundamentally occur?

options.text: The system loses energy to the environment
The system becomes entangled with the environment
The measurement apparatus collapses the state

questions.explanation: Decoherence occurs because the quantum system becomes entangled with environmental degrees of freedom. When we trace out the environment (which we cannot access), the system appears to be in a classical mixture.
